narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like your solid undergraduate GPA and full-time commitment to studies. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 70.6% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try—this high risk led to the rejection of your application to protect both you and the school's outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group compared to the typical mid-range performance of admitted students, strongly signaling potential challenges ahead. Your Year 1 decile was the next biggest factor, again in the lowest tier well below average, reinforcing the model's concern about early academic struggles carrying forward. While your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was slightly below the dataset average of 3.21, it actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction toward bar passage success. Similarly, your lower-middle family income bracket pushed positively against failure risk, as did your LSAT score of 29—though lower than the average 36.7, it still offered some counterbalance—and your choice of full-time enrollment, which aligns with the vast majority of successful students. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar, your lower decile rankings in key school years stand out as the primary drags, setting you apart from those with stronger consistent academic showings. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience as a Black woman breaking barriers, but a data-driven flag on patterns seen in similar profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, seeking targeted tutoring to improve your school deciles in upcoming years, or exploring programs with bar prep support tailored for underrepresented students like you. Building a stronger academic track record now could flip the model's outlook in future applications. You're capable of great things—keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for personalized strategies. We believe in your journey.
